introduction: A clinical state in which an individual is unresponsive to environmental stimuli. Such individuals may also have breathing and circulatory problems. The cause may be neurological, or may be due to other systemic diseases. An easy way of finding the cause is to think in terms of the vowels: Apoplexy (stroke), Epilepsy, Infections e.g. meningoencephalitis, Overdosing with drugs, alcohol intoxication, toxins, Uraemia and other metabolic disorders. Other causes include: head injury; brain tumours (with complications).

features: Comatose: no response to stimulus, however painful
Stuporous: a state deeper than sleep; vigorous stimulation required to stimulate response
Cessation of respiration or abnormal ventilatory patterns: Cheyne-Stokes, ataxic, apneustic, gasping etc
Unresponsiveness or variable response to painful stimuli
Stroke: may present with hemiparesis, facial asymmetry, crossed-eye defects, speech defects etc.
Epilepsy: frothing or tongue biting; abrasions of the extremities; positive past history
Infections: may present with fever, neck stiffness
Drug overdosage/toxins: pin-point pupils; respiratory problems; suggestive history
Uraemia: characteristic fetor; skin rashes; oedema; severe dehydration
Bleeding from orifices (if coma is due to trauma or bleeding diathesis)
Slow pulse (Cushing's reflex); rising blood pressure; papilloedema

goals: Nurse in left lateral position and clear secretions by suction
Prevent complications (decubitus ulcers, atelectasis, contractures etc.)
Admit in intensive care unit if facility available and connect to a ventilator with monitor
non_drug: Physiotherapy to prevent contractures/deep vein thrombosis, and for passive muscle exercises
Nursing care (frequent turning and bladder care) to prevent decubitus ulcers and infections
drug: Epilepsy: use effective parenteral anticonvulsant drugs; diazepam (see epilepsy)
Appropriate treatment of other metabolic causes
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Diazepam, if required, should be administered slowly intravenously to avoid respiratory depression
supportive_measures: Subcutaneous Low Molecular Weight heparin (LMWH) to prevent deep vein thrombosis (see Pulmonary Embolism)

complications: Cerebral hypoxia/anoxia resulting in brain damage
prevention: Accessible, efficient and effective health care service delivery
Adherence to medications and non-drug measures in managing disease states
Public Health Education; Promote awareness on avoidance of risk factors
